notes.note: Cicero distinguishes the elder L. Cornelius Balbus, Caesar's powerful Spanish-born confidant and agent, from his nephew of the same name. Balbus and Oppius managed Caesar's correspondence and were Cicero's chief channels for sounding out Caesar's mood.

notes.anchor: we have far more to worry about over Africa
notes.note: After Pharsalus the surviving Pompeians regrouped in the Roman province of Africa, where Cato, Metellus Scipio, and King Juba were assembling a new army. Cicero, who had abandoned the cause and returned to Italy, dreaded that their resistance would prolong the war and expose his desertion.

notes.anchor: the splendid performances of my son-in-law
notes.note: Bitter sarcasm: Cicero's son-in-law was P. Cornelius Dolabella, then tribune at Rome, whose demagogic agitation for cancellation of debts had provoked rioting. His conduct, and his treatment of Tullia, were a fresh grief on top of Cicero's political ruin.

notes.anchor: I have entered upon Galeo's inheritance
notes.note: Cicero had been named heir by one Galeo and formally accepted the bequest. The cretio was the legal act, performed within a set period, by which an instituted heir declared acceptance of an inheritance.
